By the time we caught the discrepancy, the supplier had already shipped the forty units.
The amendment had superseded revision C before the units left the factory.
When the audit began, the team had been following the superseded annex for three months.
We rejected the certificate because the wording had changed and nobody had flagged it.
They had already shipped them by then.
They had been building to it for months.
Nobody had checked the portal.
By then it had been superseded.

João, thank you for sending the non-conformance report before the meeting. Before we talk about cost, I want to walk through the sequence, because when we lay it out, I think you will see the discrepancy was shared.
I agree that the order of events matters, so let us start there. When your team began production in January, which revision had you certified the axle counters to?

Revision C. It was current when your purchase order reached us. What we did not know was that the committee had already approved an amendment in December, and that amendment had superseded revision C before we shipped a single unit.

So let me make sure I have the sequence right. By the time the amendment was published in March, you had already shipped the first batch, and you had been manufacturing to revision C for almost three months. Is that correct?

That is correct. And I will not pretend we had caught it and stayed silent — we had not. Nobody on our side had checked the portal. But your specification had not been updated either, so the discrepancy was never only ours.

Understood, and I will say so in writing. But here is where the wording matters. Our contract stipulated the standard in its current revision, and by your shipping date the current revision had changed. So which document takes precedence — the contract, or the certificate you issued?

That is the grey area, and I would rather close it than argue it. The amendment changed one provision in Annex D — the main clause had stayed the same, which is exactly why both teams read past it. The devil was in the annex, and neither of us had opened it in time.

Then let me propose this. We agree, on paper, that by the time the inspector flagged it, both sides had already been working from a superseded revision. That makes it a shared non-conformance, not a breach. Before we sign anything, I want that provision spelled out and the annex reference fixed — so the next batch never falls into the same loophole.
By the time we caught the discrepancy, the supplier had already shipped the units.
They had been manufacturing to a superseded revision for three months.
Neither side had flagged it, so the discrepancy was shared, not a breach.
Which document takes precedence here — the contract, or the standard?
Before we sign anything, I want that provision spelled out and the annex reference fixed.

João, it is Sabine. I have read your non-conformance report, and before the meeting I want to give you our side of the sequence, honestly, because I think the order of events matters more than either of us wants to admit. When your purchase order reached us in January, revision C of the standard was current, and that is the revision we built the axle counters to. What none of us knew was that the committee had already approved an amendment in December, and that amendment had superseded revision C and rewritten one provision in Annex D. It was not published on the portal until March. So by the time it appeared, we had been manufacturing to the old revision for almost three months, and we had already shipped the first batch. Now, your contract stipulated the standard in its current revision, and yes, strictly, the current revision had changed before we shipped. But nobody on either side had checked the portal, and nobody had flagged it. I am not going to pretend we had caught it and stayed silent, because we had not. What I am asking is that we agree on the timeline first, because when you read our report and yours together, you will see the discrepancy was shared. The wording left a grey area, both of us fell into it, and I would rather fix the annex reference in the contract than spend six months arguing about whose loophole it was.

Three rules, before you sign anything that refers to a technical standard, because the money is never in the clause you read, it is in the one you assumed. First rule. A standard is not a fixed thing. It has revisions, it has amendments, and an amendment can supersede the revision you certified to without changing the number on the cover. When a contract says the current revision, current means current on the day of delivery, not the day you signed. Check which revision is actually in force before you cut metal, because by the time the discrepancy shows up at the factory acceptance test, you will have built to a document that no longer exists. Second rule. Read the annexes. Everybody reads the main clause and nobody reads Annex D, and Annex D is exactly where the committee hides the change. The devil is in the details, and the details live in the fine print at the back. Third rule, and this one settles disputes. When something goes wrong, reconstruct the sequence in the past perfect, event by event, on paper. Who had done what, by when. What had already happened before the inspector opened the file. What each side had been doing while the other assumed something different. Nine times out of ten, the moment you lay the timeline out honestly, the liability stops being an argument and becomes a date. And a date is a thing two engineers can actually agree on.
